title: Diabetic Retinopathy
oneLiner: Diabetes complication causing retinal microvascular damage; leading cause of preventable blindness in working-age adults; requires screening and timely treatment.

sections.Killers First — What Will Kill Them In T:
- **Neovascular glaucoma (acute):** Sudden painful red eye + corneal oedema. Single finding: rock-hard globe on palpation (IOP >40 mmHg). Urgent IOP-lowering needed to prevent optic atrophy.
- **Acute vitreous haemorrhage + retinal detachment:** Sudden painless severe vision loss. Single finding: inability to see fundus red reflex + dark curtain. Requires immediate ophthalmology review – tractional detachment can progress rapidly.
sections.Presentation:
- **Cardinal symptoms:**
  - Asymptomatic (early disease; found on screening)
  - Blurred / fluctuating central vision (diabetic macular oedema)
  - Floaters, flashes, sudden painless vision loss (vitreous haemorrhage, retinal detachment)
  - Red, painful, cloudy eye with nausea (neovascular glaucoma)
- **Key exam findings:**
  - Direct ophthalmoscopy (dilated): microaneurysms, dot/blot haemorrhages, hard exudates, cotton wool spots, venous beading, neovascularisation at optic disc (NVD) or elsewhere (NVE)
  - In PDR: vitreous haemorrhage, tractional retinal detachment, rubeosis iridis
  - Check IOP if eye red/painful; measure visual acuity (Snellen)
sections.Differential Diagnosis:
- **Hypertensive retinopathy:** Flame haemorrhages, more arteriolar changes, less microaneurysms. **Splitter:** presence of microaneurysms favours diabetic retinopathy.
- **Retinal vein occlusion:** Unilateral, diffuse haemorrhages in all quadrants, swollen optic disc, cotton wool spots. Often in non-diabetics; consider unilateral vs bilateral.
- **Age-related macular degeneration:** Drusen, central vision loss, no microaneurysms; typically >60y, no diabetes association.
- **Ocular ischaemic syndrome:** Carotid disease; rubeosis iridis, mid-peripheral dot haemorrhages, low pressure. Suspect if asymmetric retinopathy.
sections.Investigations:
- **Bedside (first hour):**
  - Visual acuity (Snellen chart or inspection of thumb)
  - Dilated funduscopy (if not contraindicated by shallow anterior chamber)
  - IOP measurement (tonometry) if eye painful or red
- **Bloods:**
  - HbA1c (current glycaemic control)
  - Urea & creatinine, eGFR, ACR (nephropathy assessment)
  - Fasting lipid profile
- **Imaging:**
  - Fundus photography (screening programme)
  - Optical coherence tomography (OCT) for macular oedema (macular thickness)
  - Fluorescein angiography: for ischaemia, neovascularisation
- **Special tests:** None for initial ward assessment
- **Diagnostic criteria:** UK National Screening Committee grading:
  - R0 (none), R1 (background), R2 (pre-proliferative), R3 (proliferative)
  - M0 (no maculopathy), M1 (maculopathy)
- **Never-miss test:** **Dilated fundus examination** in every diabetic patient – especially with any visual symptom. Omission risks missing treatable sight-threatening disease.
sections.Management:
- **Immediate (first hour / ED):**
  - **Neovascular glaucoma:** Call ophthalmology on-call. Start oral acetazolamide 250–500 mg BD (check renal function) + topical beta-blocker (e.g., timolol 0.5% BD per local policy) + definitive PRP / anti-VEGF.
  - **Acute vitreous haemorrhage / new floaters/flashes:** Urgent ophthalmology referral. Keep patient upright, avoid straining (Valsalva). Do not give aspirin unless already prescribed.
- **Ward (first 24–72h):**
  - If incidental finding on routine exam: review HbA1c, BP, lipids. Optimise diabetes control.
  - Ensure patient registered with **Diabetic Eye Screening Programme**.
  - If admitted for other reason, request ophthalmology opinion if R2/R3/M1 or symptomatic.
- **Long-term / outpatient:**
  - Tight glycaemic control (HbA1c target <53 mmol/mol, individualised)
  - BP target <130/80 mmHg (ACEi/ARB first line)
  - Statin (atorvastatin 20–80 mg daily depending on CVD risk)
  - Annual retinal screening (DSPS) until stable; more frequent if advanced.
  - Refer for laser photocoagulation, intravitreal anti-VEGF (ranibizumab, aflibercept), or vitrectomy as indicated by ophthalmology.
- **Default Orders (if admitted):**
  - Fluid type/rate: as per diabetes management (e.g., 0.9% saline if DKA/HHS; otherwise free fluids)
  - VTE prophylaxis: standard risk assessment (LMWH if appropriate)
  - Diet: usual diabetic diet, or NBM if procedure
  - Observations: NEWS2 per local policy; blood glucose monitoring 4–6 hourly
sections.Drugs & Doses:
- **First-line agents:**
  - Intravitreal ranibizumab 0.5 mg/0.05 ml, monthly initially then PRN (ophthalmologist administered)
  - Intravitreal aflibercept 2 mg/0.05 ml, every 4–8 weeks (ophthalmologist)
  - Oral acetazolamide 250–500 mg BD (for acute glaucoma, check eGFR >20, avoid if sulphonamide allergy)
  - Topical timolol 0.5% BD (apply pressure over nasolacrimal duct to reduce systemic absorption)
- **Common alternatives:**
  - Intravitreal bevacizumab (off-label, 1.25 mg/0.05 ml) used in many centres
  - Laser photocoagulation (panretinal for PDR, focal/grid for macular oedema)
- **Key contraindications / interactions:**
  - Acetazolamide: avoid in severe renal impairment, hepatic failure, sulphonamide allergy. Hypokalaemia risk – monitor K+.
  - Intravitreal anti-VEGF: avoid in active ocular infection; caution with recent stroke/MI (theoretical risk, discuss with senior)
- **Renal/hepatic adjustment:**
  - Acetazolamide: contraindicated if eGFR <20; use half dose or avoid in cirrhosis
  - Anti-VEGF: no renal adjustment required
- **Most dangerous dose error:**
  - **Intravitreal injection – wrong drug or wrong dose** (e.g., administering systemic drug into eye). Always verify with ophthalmology before injecting.
  - **Acetazolamide – giving without baseline K+** may cause hypokalaemia; check U&E first.
sections.Complications:
- **Acute:**
  - Neovascular glaucoma
  - Vitreous haemorrhage
  - Tractional retinal detachment
  - Endophthalmitis (post-intravitreal injection)
- **Chronic:**
  - Diabetic macular oedema (persistent central vision loss)
  - Severe visual impairment / blindness
  - Rubeosis iridis (iris neovascularisation)
sections.Prognosis: - With tight control and timely treatment, sight loss is preventable. Untreated proliferative DR leads to blindness in >50% within 5 years. Screening programmes have reduced blindness rates significantly.
sections.Pearls (positive actions):
- **Always check HbA1c + BP** in any patient with diabetic retinopathy – these are the two modifiable risk factors with strongest evidence.
- **Ensure patient is enrolled in the Diabetic Eye Screening Programme** – many patients fall through the gaps.
- **Consider aspirin/antiplatelet therapy** for cardiovascular prevention – no increased risk of vitreous haemorrhage in stable retinopathy; do not stop unless ophthalmologist advises.
sections.Pitfalls (Errors To Avoid):
- **Do not assume vision loss is due to cataract** without dilating and examining fundus – may miss treatable retinopathy.
- **Do not use NSAIDs** in a patient with neovascular glaucoma (can worsen inflammation, though not a major risk; better to defer to ophthalmology).
- **Do not delay ophthalmology referral** for R2/R3/M1 – earlier treatment preserves vision.
sections.Safety Netting (If Patient Goes Home):
- **Return if:** sudden vision loss, increased floaters, flashes of light, red painful eye, loss of visual field (curtain).
- **Follow-up:** ophthalmology outpatient within 2 weeks for any new sight-threatening retinopathy; otherwise routine diabetic screening within the next 6–12 months.
sections.Escalation Criteria:
- **Call ophthalmology on-call** for: acute vision loss, suspicion of neovascular glaucoma, vitreous haemorrhage with retinal detachment, or any new floaters/flashes with no fundal view.
- **For ward patient:** If NEWS2 >5 or new confusion, treat acute diabetic emergency (DKA/HHS) first – but note eye findings for later review.

rawMarkdown:
## Diabetic Retinopathy
**One-liner:** Diabetes complication causing retinal microvascular damage; leading cause of preventable blindness in working-age adults; requires screening and timely treatment.

### Killers First — What Will Kill Them In The First Hour?
- **Neovascular glaucoma (acute):** Sudden painful red eye + corneal oedema. Single finding: rock-hard globe on palpation (IOP >40 mmHg). Urgent IOP-lowering needed to prevent optic atrophy.
- **Acute vitreous haemorrhage + retinal detachment:** Sudden painless severe vision loss. Single finding: inability to see fundus red reflex + dark curtain. Requires immediate ophthalmology review – tractional detachment can progress rapidly.

### Presentation
- **Cardinal symptoms:**
  - Asymptomatic (early disease; found on screening)
  - Blurred / fluctuating central vision (diabetic macular oedema)
  - Floaters, flashes, sudden painless vision loss (vitreous haemorrhage, retinal detachment)
  - Red, painful, cloudy eye with nausea (neovascular glaucoma)
- **Key exam findings:**
  - Direct ophthalmoscopy (dilated): microaneurysms, dot/blot haemorrhages, hard exudates, cotton wool spots, venous beading, neovascularisation at optic disc (NVD) or elsewhere (NVE)
  - In PDR: vitreous haemorrhage, tractional retinal detachment, rubeosis iridis
  - Check IOP if eye red/painful; measure visual acuity (Snellen)

### Differential Diagnosis
- **Hypertensive retinopathy:** Flame haemorrhages, more arteriolar changes, less microaneurysms. **Splitter:** presence of microaneurysms favours diabetic retinopathy.
- **Retinal vein occlusion:** Unilateral, diffuse haemorrhages in all quadrants, swollen optic disc, cotton wool spots. Often in non-diabetics; consider unilateral vs bilateral.
- **Age-related macular degeneration:** Drusen, central vision loss, no microaneurysms; typically >60y, no diabetes association.
- **Ocular ischaemic syndrome:** Carotid disease; rubeosis iridis, mid-peripheral dot haemorrhages, low pressure. Suspect if asymmetric retinopathy.

### Investigations
- **Bedside (first hour):**
  - Visual acuity (Snellen chart or inspection of thumb)
  - Dilated funduscopy (if not contraindicated by shallow anterior chamber)
  - IOP measurement (tonometry) if eye painful or red
- **Bloods:**
  - HbA1c (current glycaemic control)
  - Urea & creatinine, eGFR, ACR (nephropathy assessment)
  - Fasting lipid profile
- **Imaging:**
  - Fundus photography (screening programme)
  - Optical coherence tomography (OCT) for macular oedema (macular thickness)
  - Fluorescein angiography: for ischaemia, neovascularisation
- **Special tests:** None for initial ward assessment
- **Diagnostic criteria:** UK National Screening Committee grading:
  - R0 (none), R1 (background), R2 (pre-proliferative), R3 (proliferative)
  - M0 (no maculopathy), M1 (maculopathy)
- **Never-miss test:** **Dilated fundus examination** in every diabetic patient – especially with any visual symptom. Omission risks missing treatable sight-threatening disease.

### Management
- **Immediate (first hour / ED):**
  - **Neovascular glaucoma:** Call ophthalmology on-call. Start oral acetazolamide 250–500 mg BD (check renal function) + topical beta-blocker (e.g., timolol 0.5% BD per local policy) + definitive PRP / anti-VEGF.
  - **Acute vitreous haemorrhage / new floaters/flashes:** Urgent ophthalmology referral. Keep patient upright, avoid straining (Valsalva). Do not give aspirin unless already prescribed.
- **Ward (first 24–72h):**
  - If incidental finding on routine exam: review HbA1c, BP, lipids. Optimise diabetes control.
  - Ensure patient registered with **Diabetic Eye Screening Programme**.
  - If admitted for other reason, request ophthalmology opinion if R2/R3/M1 or symptomatic.
- **Long-term / outpatient:**
  - Tight glycaemic control (HbA1c target <53 mmol/mol, individualised)
  - BP target <130/80 mmHg (ACEi/ARB first line)
  - Statin (atorvastatin 20–80 mg daily depending on CVD risk)
  - Annual retinal screening (DSPS) until stable; more frequent if advanced.
  - Refer for laser photocoagulation, intravitreal anti-VEGF (ranibizumab, aflibercept), or vitrectomy as indicated by ophthalmology.
- **Default Orders (if admitted):**
  - Fluid type/rate: as per diabetes management (e.g., 0.9% saline if DKA/HHS; otherwise free fluids)
  - VTE prophylaxis: standard risk assessment (LMWH if appropriate)
  - Diet: usual diabetic diet, or NBM if procedure
  - Observations: NEWS2 per local policy; blood glucose monitoring 4–6 hourly

### Drugs & Doses
- **First-line agents:**
  - Intravitreal ranibizumab 0.5 mg/0.05 ml, monthly initially then PRN (ophthalmologist administered)
  - Intravitreal aflibercept 2 mg/0.05 ml, every 4–8 weeks (ophthalmologist)
  - Oral acetazolamide 250–500 mg BD (for acute glaucoma, check eGFR >20, avoid if sulphonamide allergy)
  - Topical timolol 0.5% BD (apply pressure over nasolacrimal duct to reduce systemic absorption)
- **Common alternatives:**
  - Intravitreal bevacizumab (off-label, 1.25 mg/0.05 ml) used in many centres
  - Laser photocoagulation (panretinal for PDR, focal/grid for macular oedema)
- **Key contraindications / interactions:**
  - Acetazolamide: avoid in severe renal impairment, hepatic failure, sulphonamide allergy. Hypokalaemia risk – monitor K+.
  - Intravitreal anti-VEGF: avoid in active ocular infection; caution with recent stroke/MI (theoretical risk, discuss with senior)
- **Renal/hepatic adjustment:**
  - Acetazolamide: contraindicated if eGFR <20; use half dose or avoid in cirrhosis
  - Anti-VEGF: no renal adjustment required
- **Most dangerous dose error:**
  - **Intravitreal injection – wrong drug or wrong dose** (e.g., administering systemic drug into eye). Always verify with ophthalmology before injecting.
  - **Acetazolamide – giving without baseline K+** may cause hypokalaemia; check U&E first.

### Complications
- **Acute:**
  - Neovascular glaucoma
  - Vitreous haemorrhage
  - Tractional retinal detachment
  - Endophthalmitis (post-intravitreal injection)
- **Chronic:**
  - Diabetic macular oedema (persistent central vision loss)
  - Severe visual impairment / blindness
  - Rubeosis iridis (iris neovascularisation)

### Prognosis
- With tight control and timely treatment, sight loss is preventable. Untreated proliferative DR leads to blindness in >50% within 5 years. Screening programmes have reduced blindness rates significantly.

### Pearls (positive actions)
- **Always check HbA1c + BP** in any patient with diabetic retinopathy – these are the two modifiable risk factors with strongest evidence.
- **Ensure patient is enrolled in the Diabetic Eye Screening Programme** – many patients fall through the gaps.
- **Consider aspirin/antiplatelet therapy** for cardiovascular prevention – no increased risk of vitreous haemorrhage in stable retinopathy; do not stop unless ophthalmologist advises.

### Pitfalls (Errors To Avoid)
- **Do not assume vision loss is due to cataract** without dilating and examining fundus – may miss treatable retinopathy.
- **Do not use NSAIDs** in a patient with neovascular glaucoma (can worsen inflammation, though not a major risk; better to defer to ophthalmology).
- **Do not delay ophthalmology referral** for R2/R3/M1 – earlier treatment preserves vision.

### Safety Netting (If Patient Goes Home)
- **Return if:** sudden vision loss, increased floaters, flashes of light, red painful eye, loss of visual field (curtain).
- **Follow-up:** ophthalmology outpatient within 2 weeks for any new sight-threatening retinopathy; otherwise routine diabetic screening within the next 6–12 months.

### Escalation Criteria
- **Call ophthalmology on-call** for: acute vision loss, suspicion of neovascular glaucoma, vitreous haemorrhage with retinal detachment, or any new floaters/flashes with no fundal view.
- **For ward patient:** If NEWS2 >5 or new confusion, treat acute diabetic emergency (DKA/HHS) first – but note eye findings for later review.
